metadata.title: The Obvious Answer (And Why It’s Wrong)
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.

tags: The Obvious Answer (And Why It’s Wrong)
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The obvious answer, that approvals just reward good companies, turns out to be wrong. Stocks earned about two percent returns in five days after a bureaucrat said yes. Half the companies showed exactly zero price movement, so approval alone isn't the driver. The favoritism vanished whenever oversight existed, which betrays the real mechanism.

artifactLines: The obvious answer, that approvals just reward good companies, turns out to be wrong.
Stocks earned about two percent returns in five days after a bureaucrat said yes.
Half the companies showed exactly zero price movement, so approval alone isn't the driver.
The favoritism vanished whenever oversight existed, which betrays the real mechanism.

beats.narration_text: The obvious answer felt wrong to me too, and I want to see why the data forces a different reading. Help me interrogate the finding. Explain: (1) how a 2% return over just five days after a regulatory 'yes' counts as an involuntary confession by the market, (2) why the favoritism vanishing precisely when oversight existed rules out the innocent explanation, and (3) what the missing piece is that keeps this from being fully explained by companies simply deserving approval.
